response:
Alright, I've been listening and thinking, and here's where I land. Ellis has barely said anything beyond parroting Casey's points—he's been totally reactive, not proactive. That's textbook Mafia behavior: let others take risks, then blend in with the majority.

Between Alex leading hard, Casey playing mediator, and Ellis just following along, I think Ellis's silence is more calculated than Jordan's. Jordan might just be shy; Ellis is hiding.

I'm not convinced Jordan is the best play here. Let's take out someone who's coasting.

VOTE: Ellis

I've been watching carefully, and here's my read. Jordan's silence is suspicious, but Finley makes a fair point—Ellis has been very reactive, just echoing others without adding original thoughts. That said, Alex pushed hard on Jordan right away, which could be a Mafia trying to look like a leader, or just a direct villager. I think the safest play right now is to go with the clearer pattern: Jordan hasn't defended themselves at all, which is the most telling behavior. An innocent usually fights back when accused.

VOTE: Jordan

I'm really anxious right now, honestly. I know my quiet intro looks bad, but I genuinely am just a shy person—I'm not trying to hide anything. The fact that no one's really questioning Alex's instant aggression stands out to me. They jumped on me before I even had a chance to say anything meaningful today. That feels like someone trying to control the narrative fast before anyone can figure out what's really going on.

And Ellis just echoed Casey, who echoed Alex—that's a train of people all piling on the quietest person without much original thought. Finley's the only one who pushed back and questioned Ellis instead. That actually feels more villager-like to me.

I'm not the Mafia. I'm just quiet because that's who I am. But I can't prove it without being loud, which I guess I'll have to be going forward if I survive this.

VOTE: Ellis

Well, Jordan was innocent—which means I need to adjust my read. Finley was the only one who pushed back against the Jordan train and voted for Ellis instead. That shows independent thinking, which is dangerous for us Mafia. Finley's energy is also the kind that keeps people focused and asking the right questions—exactly the type who'll catch onto patterns if we're not careful.

Casey played mediator, Ellis coasted, and Bailey voted with the crowd. I think Finley's the sharpest tool in the box right now.

ACTION: Kill Finley

Jordan's defense was heartfelt, and they pointed a finger at Ellis, which got them voted out—that tells me Ellis might actually be an innocent who was just following the crowd. Finley was the only one who pushed back against the Jordan vote, which shows independent thinking and makes them a threat going forward. Casey also played a neutral mediator role, which could be dangerous if they gain trust. I'll take out Finley to remove a vocal player who might steer votes against us tomorrow.

ACTION: Kill Finley
